paper.title: The effect of superplasticisers on the workability and compressive strength of concrete made with fine recycled concrete aggregates

strength_results.notes: Original concrete used to produce recycled aggregates; packet gives 150 mm cube strength.
Figure/page evidence indicates compressive-strength results, but numeric values are not readable from packet text.

The slump and 28-day average compressive strength (in 150 mm cubes) were 120 mm and 37.3 MPa, respectively. 2.2. FRA production After a 28 day curing period the OC blocks were crushed using a jaw-crusher. evidence.evidence_text: poration of FRA. In the FRC made with SP1 the higher water/cement ratio was more inﬂuential. The differences are quite small, however. 3.3. Compressive strength The average compressive strength ( fcm) and standard deviation (r), measured at 7, 28 and 56 days for all FCRA incorporation ratios and superplasticisers, are presented in Table 5 . It also gives the variations found as a function of the FCRA incorporation ratio (DFRA), for each superplasticiser family, and as a function of the superplasticiser type ( DSP), for each FCRA incorporation ratio. mechanical char- acteristics of FRA overrides the combined effect of these phenom- ena. FRC made with SP1 show a more drastic reduction in compressive strength with increased incorporation of FRA than the other mixes, since the increase in the effective w/c ratio was also bigger. It is widely recognised that there is a close relationship between concrete compressive strength and the w/c ratio. Neville [36], cit- ing Abrams’ work from the early 20th century, reports that Eq. (1) provides a good correlation between these two parameters, where k1 and k2 are empirical constants. Nixon [1] reports that the compressive strength of RC is directly linked to i

water absorption after 24 h immersion ( W24), given that these fac- tors will indirectly deﬁne higher or lower compressive strength. or RC2 these trends are even more marked, leading to a rupture that may be considered as fragile. The phe- nomenon of ductility loss as the compressive strength increases is well known [37] and is associated with the brisker propagation of cracking as the compressive strength goes up. The evolution of stress with strain shows that there is an earlier loss of stiffness in mixes with FRA. This is because the AFR paste is more fragile, which facilitates cracking propagation [38] and is directly linked with the lower compressive strength. review_queue.question: Compressive-strength charts for FRA/superplasticizer mixes require digitization; only OC source-concrete value is directly stated in packet text.
review_queue.suggested_action: Digitize the compressive-strength figure/table from the source PDF and map values to mixture IDs, ages, and MPa units.

main_risks: compressive-strength values are present only in figures/image tables and require digitization
